title: Seabright Rising
summary: Amara Vale, a scientist returned to her coastal hometown of Seabright in 2035, leads community-centered adaptation efforts — mangrove restoration, raised promenades, and microgrids — while navigating pressures from private developer Marco Voss, local politics, and activist networks. The player builds alliances (Eli, Hal, Miriam, Rosa), negotiates ethical trade-offs around funding and displacement, and chooses between engineering-ecology collaboration, conditional private partnerships, or pure grassroots mobilization. Branches explore different governance and technical outcomes; endings vary in texture but all represent a steady upward arc of resilience, trust, and institutional capacity.

chapter_structure.narrative_focus: I open the story on the Seabright Boardwalk after a late morning survey, cataloging salt stains and algae lines, trading practical updates with Eli and being called into Rosa's café. I sketch an early plan to rally neighbors around mangroves, microgrids, and raised promenades while admitting survivor's guilt and an urge to shoulder responsibility.

At the Beacon climate center I review sensor data, coordinate volunteer plans with Hal and Miriam, and receive Marco Voss's glossy development invite. I decide to call a town meeting to balance Seabright's character with needed capacity, and ask Eli to check foundation stress curves with me.

At Town Hall I present community-led plans while Marco pitches a private development. The council is poised to authorize exploratory deals; I must endorse a path publicly that will pivot Seabright toward private partnership, engineering-ecology collaboration, or full grassroots resistance.

major_decision_point.description: Which path do I endorse publicly at the meeting?

options.label: Work closely with Eli and prioritize pragmatic engineering + ecology
Accept Marco’s partnership under strict terms
options.consequence: Private capital route; fast infrastructure with compromises

options.label: Reject corporate deals; escalate grassroots mobilization
options.consequence: Pure community-driven push for equity-first measures

chapter_structure.narrative_focus: I work with Eli at the Mangrove Restoration Site installing saplings and sensor nodes, and we build trust as volunteers and elders contribute. We plan modular seawalls integrated with mangrove channels and prepare a test run for raised promenade segments.

At the Beacon we reconcile ideals with invoices, debating mangrove saplings, raised boardwalks, sensors, stipends, and maintenance. The decision determines funding focus and who we must persuade to implement the plan.

major_decision_point.description: Which funding and focus do I recommend?

options.label: Prioritize mangrove + promenade scaffolding
options.consequence: Deep ecological resilience; slower economic payoff

options.label: Prioritize grants and retrofits for small businesses now
options.consequence: Immediate relief; harder to scale ecological measures

options.label: Push for microgrid pilot requiring legal work
options.consequence: Potential systemic change; high negotiation risk

chapter_structure.narrative_focus: We complete the first raised promenade section, plant mangroves, and see community momentum translate into micro-loans and a maintenance plan. The pilot proves effective; Eli and I quietly commit to keep building together.

I meet Marco for an exploratory sit-down about a modernized Seabright, weighing immediate capital and jobs against displacement. Eli acts as a technical sounding board while Miriam protests outside. I probe contract terms and consider whether to leverage constraints or resist private capital.
At the Beacon we parse Marco's draft contract, debating indemnities, easements, oversight, relocation funds, and maintenance endowments. The negotiation will shape infrastructure and governance.

major_decision_point.description: How do we negotiate Marco’s contract?

options.label: Insist on strict redlines and oversight committees
Accept the partnership to get fast infrastructure
Reject and mobilize local financing and bonds
options.consequence: Slower, grassroots funding; greater local control

chapter_structure.narrative_focus: We secure enforceable oversight, maintenance funds, and rehousing budgets in a rewritten contract. The pilot seawall is paired with managed restoration corridors and a maintenance endowment run by a new community trust, balancing infrastructure speed with community safeguards.

I lead a grassroots escalation: marches, a citizens' charter, micro-investment cooperatives, and a local bond issue. We occupy the plaza and demand public financing and legal protections before private deals; Marco's team retaliates with legal threats.

We craft community bonds, cooperative ownership models, and bylaws to guarantee affordable space and maintenance endowments. The choice is whether to push for uncompromising equitable ownership, negotiate a monitored hybrid with private partners, or pilot microfinance for immediate relief.

major_decision_point.description: Which institutional path will we endorse?

options.label: Double down on community bonds and bylaws
Negotiate a monitored hybrid with private partners under strict bylaw safeguards
Aim for a quick microfinance pilot for small business retrofits before larger projects

chapter_structure.narrative_focus: The community bond narrowly passes; funds are allocated to retrofits, a phased mangrove corridor, and a modest seawall. Some projects stall, some shops struggle but receive cooperative loans, and a modular foundation pilot led by Eli demonstrates pragmatic progress and deeper community self-determination.

Months later, across paths, I review a stitched map of Seabright showing raised promenades, mangrove corridors, modular seawalls, rehousing pockets, and microgrid nodes. The town bears scars and new structures; policies, funding streams, and community institutions are in place, and my relationship with Eli has deepened through shared work.
chapter_structure.entry_condition: From Chapter 6 or Chapter 9 or Chapter 12

chapter_structure.narrative_focus: Where speed ruled, a seawall is built quickly and saves lives in storms but costs some historic storefronts and displaces some residents. Oversight clauses and rehousing endowments mitigate outcomes; the town is measurably safer but carries the cost of compromise.
chapter_structure.entry_condition: From Chapter 8, Option B or From Chapter 11, Option C

chapter_structure.narrative_focus: At dusk after a storm I stand with Eli on the raised boardwalk and reflect on shared responsibility, matured community trust, maintenance crews, mangrove silt-holding corridors, and emergent hybrid governance blending private resources with public accountability. The final image is imperfect but durable and upward.
chapter_structure.entry_condition: From Chapter 8, Option C or From Chapter 14

remarks: Chapters 1–3 are common, splitting at Chapter 3 into three main routes: Route A (Chapters 4–6, split at 5), Route B (Chapters 7–9, split at 8), and Route C (Chapters 10–12, split at 11). Chapters 13–15 serve as epilogues/convergent outcomes accessible from multiple upstream choices. All chapters are first-person and follow a steadily rising emotional arc.
